findings.title: Corrupt alert-state.json blocks the snapshot commit for the entire day
findings.description: The 'Strip methodHistory from alert-state' step has no `continue-on-error: true` and exits with code 1 when alert-state.json is corrupt JSON (the Tag 179 F-CI-005 v2 fail-loud fix). Because GitHub Actions skips subsequent non-`if: always()`/`if: failure()` steps after a step failure, a corrupt alert-state.json from any cause (interrupted prior run, race, partial write) prevents the 'Commit Snapshots + Alert-State' step from running — discarding ~4 hours of fresh snapshot pulls, modes-report, screener.html, picks-history, methods-history and walk-forward results that have nothing to do with alert-state. The pipeline-health and pages-deploy steps are also skipped, and the next day's detect-changes will face the same corrupt file (since it was never replaced) so the failure is sticky until manual intervention.
findings.mechanism: Job DAG: 'Strip methodHistory' (no continue-on-error) exits 1 on parse failure → subsequent default steps are skipped (no `if: always()` on commit step) → snapshots/picks-history/methods-history left only in the runner's ephemeral workspace → runner is torn down → day's data is lost. Recovery requires a human to manually delete or repair alert-state.json on main; until then every daily run repeats the same failure because the corrupt file is what main has.
findings.reproducer: Truncate alert-state.json to '{' on main, push, dispatch daily-pull. The pull, all reports, and commit step all do not execute even though Yahoo Pull succeeded.
findings.impact: Up to 4h of compute and ~15k snapshot files lost; vintage continuity gap in picks-history/methods-history breaks the walk-forward-perf 84-day window. F-CI-005 (originally placed the health check AFTER commit specifically to preserve snapshots on health-check failure) — but the alert-state strip step is BEFORE commit and re-introduces the same hazard the F-CI-005 placement was meant to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `continue-on-error: true` to the strip step and let the commit step run with the still-corrupt alert-state (subsequent detect-changes runs already handle missing/corrupt state by reinitializing), OR (b) on parse failure, write a fresh empty `{}` to alert-state.json AFTER backing up the corrupt one and exit 0, so the day's commit proceeds (the backup file provides the audit trail F-CI-005 v2 wanted). Option (b) preserves both fail-loud visibility (a `.corrupt.<ts>.json` lands in the commit, visible in git log + Discord) and data preservation.
findings.compound_hint: Same family as Tag 179 F-CI-005 (commit ordering) — the alert-state strip step needs the same 'data first, diagnostics second' ordering applied to it.

findings.title: `git rebase --strategy-option theirs` against origin/main can silently drop the just-pushed snapshots' file content
findings.description: The F-CI-001 v2 (Tag 179) retry block uses `git rebase --autostash --strategy-option theirs origin/main`. In rebase semantics, 'theirs' refers to the BRANCH BEING REBASED ONTO (origin/main) — the OPPOSITE of merge's 'theirs'. So on any path-level conflict between the local snapshot commit and remote main, the LOCAL CHANGES are discarded in favor of remote. The commit SHA verification only checks the commit OBJECT survived rebase — but the rebased commit can have an empty / no-op diff after conflict resolution favored origin/main. `git push` then succeeds publishing an empty commit, the workflow reports green, and snapshots vanish. The same hazard exists in monthly-sec-xbrl.yml line 83.
findings.mechanism: Workflow A pushes snapshots/ABC.json with new asOf. Workflow B (concurrent dispatch) modifies snapshots/ABC.json. A's commit SHA gets rebased onto B's main with -X theirs → A's content replaced by B's content for ABC.json. SHA survives (commit object intact), so the post-rebase `if ! git rev-list HEAD | grep -q` check passes. Push succeeds with a now-empty commit. Logged message 'Pushed on attempt 1' is misleading.
findings.reproducer: Two parallel runs: dispatch monthly-sec-xbrl manually while daily-pull is in the commit phase. The first to finish wins, but the loser's commit gets rebased with --strategy-option theirs and silently empties.
findings.impact: Daily snapshots lost on rebase conflict — the EXACT failure mode F-CI-001 v2 was meant to prevent. Probability is low (concurrency group serializes scheduled runs) but non-zero on the 1st of the month when daily + monthly overlap, and on workflow_dispatch (which now uses a per-run group per Tag 219a F-218c-03, so it CAN run concurrently with the scheduled daily push).
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) drop `--strategy-option theirs` (let rebase fail noisily on conflict, which the existing abort-and-cherry-pick fallback then handles correctly), or (b) verify after rebase that the commit's tree-diff is non-empty: `git diff HEAD~1 HEAD --quiet && { echo '::error::rebase emptied our commit'; git reset --hard ${COMMIT_SHA}; }` before pushing. Option (a) is simpler and matches the cherry-pick recovery path that's already battle-tested.
findings.compound_hint: Pairs with F-CI-005 (concurrency group race) — together they form the daily+monthly 1st-of-month data-loss window.

findings.title: Pages deploy step force-pushes gh-pages while repo Pages config is main+Jekyll — drift
findings.description: Per the audit brief's context note: 'workflow force-pushes to gh-pages, but Pages config is actually set to main+Jekyll'. The workflow's Deploy step builds _site/ and `git push --force origin gh-pages` (line 608). If GitHub Pages is actually serving from main/Jekyll, the gh-pages branch updates are ignored entirely and the live site is whatever Jekyll renders from main — meaning none of the new reports (modes-report.html, screener.html, dashboard.html, outputs/*) are served despite the workflow reporting '✓ GitHub Pages deployed on attempt 1'. Also: the `permissions: pages: write, id-token: write` block (lines 10-11) is appropriate for the official `actions/deploy-pages@v4` action, not for the manual git push approach used here — so those permissions are dead weight that incorrectly implies the modern Pages API path is in use.
findings.mechanism: Workflow writes to gh-pages → Pages serves from main → reports never reach users. The mismatch is invisible from CI (the push succeeds) and only visible by visiting the live URL. The force-push also blows away any history on gh-pages every run.
findings.reproducer: Visit the GitHub Pages URL for the repo after a successful run; compare the served modes-report.html version to the one committed to gh-pages. They will mismatch if Pages source is main.
findings.impact: Users see stale or wrong reports; the entire deploy step is theatre. Also, force-push to gh-pages destroys any audit trail of past deploys.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) align Pages config to gh-pages (Settings → Pages → Source: gh-pages / root) AND keep the current workflow, or (b) migrate to the official `actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3` + `actions/deploy-pages@v4` pattern (uses the existing `pages: write` + `id-token: write` permissions correctly, no gh-pages branch needed). Option (b) is the GitHub-recommended path and removes the force-push.

findings.title: FINNHUB_API_KEY missing only warns transitively — refresh-universe silently runs degraded
findings.description: `Refresh Universe` reads `FINNHUB_API_KEY` from secrets and runs with `continue-on-error: true`. There is no pre-flight check that the secret is set (unlike `DISCORD_WEBHOOK` which has explicit `if [ -z ... ]` guards at lines 387 and 460). If the secret expires, gets rotated, or is removed (no email notification from Finnhub or GitHub), refresh-universe.js falls back to SEC/Wikipedia/OTC sources only, the Verify Watchlist Sanity step (>=200 floor) still passes because the existing 15,734-stock watchlist persists, and the universe quietly stagnates — no new IPOs, no new listings. There is no `vars`/secret existence check anywhere in the file.
findings.mechanism: Finnhub key rotated → refresh-universe.js exits with error fetching from Finnhub → continue-on-error swallows → watchlist already exists with 15k tickers → Verify Watchlist Sanity passes → pipeline continues normally → universe stops growing → newly-IPO'd tickers (the Tag 227a fix target) never appear → silent regression on the exact symptom Tag 227a was meant to fix.
findings.reproducer: Set FINNHUB_API_KEY to an invalid value in repo secrets, dispatch daily-pull. Step shows red checkmark (continue-on-error) but watchlist unchanged.
findings.impact: Newly-IPO'd tickers silently never enter the watchlist; Tag 227a's MAX_UNIVERSE=25000 universe headroom is never exercised because the Finnhub source — the primary new-listing feed — is dead.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-flight existence check like the DISCORD_WEBHOOK pattern: `if [ -z "$FINNHUB_API_KEY" ]; then echo '::warning::FINNHUB_API_KEY not set — refresh-universe will run without Finnhub source'; fi`. Better: have refresh-universe.js exit non-zero (caught by continue-on-error) and add a sanity step that asserts the watchlist GREW (lastUniverseRefresh timestamp moved forward) at least weekly.
findings.compound_hint: Pairs with F-CI-007 (watchlist size floor=200 is too generous to catch degradation).

findings.title: workflow_dispatch concurrency bypass plus monthly-sec-xbrl `main-push` group creates an unintended race on the 1st of the month
findings.description: Tag 219a (F-218c-03) gave manual daily-pull dispatches a per-run concurrency group (`manual-{run_id}`) so operators can re-run while a scheduled pull is in flight. monthly-sec-xbrl.yml unconditionally uses `main-push`. On the 1st of the month, a manual daily-pull dispatch can run concurrently with the scheduled monthly-sec-xbrl. Both touch the repo's main branch via the retry-rebase commit step. The cron schedule of the daily-pull is `0 2 * * *` and monthly is `0 8 1 * *` — non-overlapping by design, but the cron-firing-3-4h-late note in the brief means daily's 240-min budget could push its commit phase to 09-10 UTC, right when monthly is committing. With the manual-dispatch escape hatch, three concurrent pushers are possible (scheduled-daily on main-push, manual-daily on manual-{id}, scheduled-monthly on main-push). The `--strategy-option theirs` (F-CI-002) makes the resulting commit-order race a data-loss hazard rather than a benign retry.
findings.mechanism: Manual daily-pull dispatch + scheduled monthly-sec-xbrl both in the commit phase → fetch+rebase race → one rebase wins on push, the other re-fetches, rebases with theirs, and may end up empty. Concurrency group does NOT serialize them because they're in different groups.
findings.reproducer: Manually dispatch daily-pull while monthly-sec-xbrl is running on the 1st of the month (or simulate by triggering both via gh CLI back-to-back).
findings.impact: Silent data loss on the 1st of the month (the only day both pipelines push). The exact failure shape is one rebase emptying the other's commit (see F-CI-002).
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) keep daily-pull's dispatch escape hatch but block monthly-sec-xbrl from running concurrently with a manual daily-pull by checking workflow run status before the commit phase, OR (b) merge to a single push-mutex: have monthly-sec-xbrl ALSO use `manual-{run_id}` for workflow_dispatch and have BOTH workflows always serialize on `main-push` for scheduled runs. Then on the 1st, scheduled daily + scheduled monthly serialize correctly, and manual dispatches use their own group. The current asymmetry is the trap.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-CI-002 (theirs strategy on rebase). Together they form the data-loss window on month-end runs.

findings.title: monthly-sec-xbrl.yml lacks fetch-depth:0, npm cache, step timeouts, and continue-on-error guards that daily-pull has
findings.description: monthly-sec-xbrl.yml drifts from daily-pull's hardened patterns: (a) `actions/checkout@v4` (line 31) does NOT set `fetch-depth: 0` — Tag 179 F-CI-004's stated requirement for reliable git-log/rebase operations is violated. The Tag 218 F-218c-02 cherry-pick-back logic added at line 86-89 depends on `git rev-list HEAD` finding the original SHA after rebase — with a shallow clone of depth 1, the local commit's parent history may be entirely missing, causing the rebase to behave unpredictably or the rev-list check to silently miss the SHA. (b) Setup Node (lines 33-36) has no `cache: 'npm'` — wastes 30-90s every run. (c) `Pull SEC XBRL` (lines 41-47) has no `timeout-minutes` and no `continue-on-error`. With pull-sec-xbrl.js historically taking 30-60 minutes and the job budget being 90, a single hung HTTP request can eat the entire budget — no per-step timeout means the SIGKILL happens at the job level, leaving the manifest in whatever state pull-sec-xbrl.js last wrote. (d) `Verify SEC Coverage` (lines 50-61) only emits `::warning::` when `n_ok < 100` — there is no hard-fail path, so an empty/broken pull commits a useless manifest and the workflow reports green.
findings.mechanism: Shallow clone + Tag 218 cherry-pick-back logic → if rebase loses the commit, the rev-list check may return false even when the SHA is reachable (depth limit), triggering an unnecessary reset-and-cherry-pick. OR the cherry-pick fails because parent commits aren't fetched. Soft Verify Coverage → manifest with `n_ok: 0` lands in main, hides the failure.
findings.reproducer: Force an empty SEC pull (e.g., temporarily block sec.gov in pull-sec-xbrl.js for testing). Workflow commits the empty manifest with only a warning.
findings.impact: Monthly SEC data updates can silently fail without hard-fail signal; the gh-pages / downstream consumers see stale SEC fundamentals for up to a month before the next monthly run.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror daily-pull's hardening: add `fetch-depth: 0` to checkout, add `cache: 'npm'` to setup-node, add `timeout-minutes: 75` and `continue-on-error: false` to Pull SEC XBRL, and make Verify SEC Coverage hard-fail (`exit 1`) when `n_ok < 100 && n_total > 1000` (clear broken-pull signal).
findings.compound_hint: Pairs with F-CI-002 — the cherry-pick-back logic was ported from daily-pull but the prerequisite fetch-depth:0 was not.

findings.title: No fallback path if GitHub silently skips a scheduled cron run entirely
findings.description: Per the brief: 'is there fallback logic if cron is silently skipped entirely?' The answer is no. The daily-pull cron is `0 2 * * *` with only `workflow_dispatch: {}` as backup. GitHub silently drops scheduled events on the free plan when runners are over-subscribed (most commonly during US business hours UTC mornings). There is no canary/heartbeat job that checks 'did today's run actually start?' If a run is skipped entirely, the next run looks normal — but vintage gaps accumulate in picks-history/methods-history, breaking the 84-day walk-forward window invisibly. The cron-firing-3-4h-late symptom noted in the brief suggests this is already an active risk in this account.
findings.mechanism: GitHub skips cron → no run created → no failure notification → next day's run sees a 48h gap between picks-history snapshots → walk-forward-perf's 84-day window has a hole → forward-return computations silently treat the gap as zero-return → method-effectiveness scores get noisier without any error signal.
findings.reproducer: Disable the workflow for 24h via UI, re-enable, observe no alert about the missing run.
findings.impact: Vintage continuity gaps in picks-history are the silent killer of walk-forward backtest validity. The pipeline-health-check.js script (line 577) doesn't check 'when did this workflow last run?'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a daily-heartbeat external monitor (e.g., a separate scheduled workflow that just curls the GitHub Actions API for the last successful daily-pull run and Discord-alerts if it's > 36h old), OR schedule the daily-pull twice (e.g., `0 2 * * *` and `0 14 * * *`) with an early-exit guard if the morning run already committed today's snapshots. The latter doubles cost but is self-contained.

findings.title: Only Yahoo Pull has a per-step timeout — all other ~25 steps can individually hang and eat the 240-min job budget
findings.description: Tag 219 raised job timeout-minutes to 240 to give the downstream chain ~75min of headroom after Yahoo Pull. Only `Run Yahoo Pull` has its own `timeout-minutes: 165`. All other steps — including ones that hit the network (Pull Historical Prices with PRICE_CONCURRENCY=20, Pull Earnings-Calendar with EARNINGS_CONCURRENCY=20, Refresh Universe, Refresh FX-Rates, Detect Changes Discord webhook) — have no per-step timeout. A single hung HTTPS request in any of these can consume the remaining ~75min job budget, killing the workflow at SIGKILL with whatever state happens to be on disk. Tag 215f explicitly lowered Yahoo's concurrency 20->8 because 20 produced 82% rate-limit failures — but `PRICE_CONCURRENCY: '20'` (line 416) and `EARNINGS_CONCURRENCY: '20'` (line 408) still hit the SAME Yahoo CDN/Cloudflare edge at the same rate that broke Yahoo Pull. Pull Historical Prices is documented to need ~10-20min normally but has no upper bound.
findings.mechanism: Concurrent 20-way requests to query2.finance.yahoo.com from Pull Historical Prices → Cloudflare throttle → some requests hang (vs error) → no per-step timeout → step blocks for hours → job-level SIGKILL → commit step never runs → day's snapshots lost (Yahoo Pull already finished and wrote to disk, but no commit happened).
findings.reproducer: Inject a 30-min sleep into pull-historical-prices.js's per-ticker callback. Workflow hangs at that step until job timeout.
findings.impact: Pull Historical Prices or Pull Earnings hang -> no commit -> day's Yahoo Pull data lost. Lower probability than F-CI-001 but same data-loss shape.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `timeout-minutes: 25` to Pull Historical Prices, `timeout-minutes: 15` to Pull Earnings-Calendar, `timeout-minutes: 10` to Refresh Universe, `timeout-minutes: 5` to Refresh FX-Rates, `timeout-minutes: 5` to each generate-* step. Lower PRICE_CONCURRENCY and EARNINGS_CONCURRENCY from 20 to 8 to match the Tag 215f Yahoo lesson. Per-step timeouts honour the continue-on-error setting — a timed-out continue-on-error:true step is just skipped, preserving the pipeline.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-CI-001 (commit-blocking) — both lose a day's snapshots via different paths.
